claimText: True Polar Wander (TPW) refers to the rotation of the solid Earth, comprising the crust and mantle, about the liquid outer core.
evidenceExcerpt: True Polar Wander (TPW) involves the rotation of the solid Earth (crust and mantle) about the liquid outer core to maintain rotational balance.
examUse: Statement-based question on the geophysical definition of True Polar Wander.
distractorHooks: Rotation of the inner core relative to the mantle
Movement of the magnetic poles rather than the solid Earth

claimText: Human activities, such as large-scale dam construction, can drive True Polar Wander by shifting the Earth's rotational axis.
evidenceExcerpt: A recent study revealed that dam construction has shifted Earth’s rotational axis by over 1 meter since 1835 i.e., they have driven True Polar Wander (TPW).
examUse: Statement-based question identifying anthropogenic drivers of Earth's rotational shifts.
distractorHooks: TPW is exclusively driven by natural phenomena like tectonic shifts
Dam construction only affects local seismicity, not the rotational axis

claimText: The movement of mass from the poles to the equator due to melting ice flattens the Earth slightly, which slows down its spin and leads to longer days.
evidenceExcerpt: Shifting poles move mass from the poles to the equator, making the Earth slightly more flattened and slowing its spin, which results in longer days.
examUse: Cause-and-effect question on the impact of climate change and ice melt on the length of a day.
distractorHooks: Movement of mass to the equator speeds up Earth's spin
Melting ice results in shorter days
Mass shifts make the Earth more perfectly spherical
